category_title: Ambulant child with Autism and severe learning difficulties using AAC and behavioral protests
minimal_context: The teacher is prompting Adam in the lounge to transition outside for his horse riding activity.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Adam, it is time to transition and go outside for horse riding.

STUDENT: (Adam pushes the horse riding picture away and shows a distressed facial expression.)

TUTOR: We need to go outside now. Let's get your riding boots.

STUDENT: (Adam drops down and sits on the floor in the lounge, refusing to move.)

TUTOR: Come on, Adam, you need to stand up.

STUDENT: (As the teacher comes near him, Adam spits at them, showing an intense protest against the activity.)

options.A: Provide an opportunity for Adam to work through the challenging transition and explain his reasoning and rationale for his protest.
options.B: Ask a guiding question about the underlying transition schedule and direct Adam to use his AAC pictures to communicate.
options.C: Tell Adam, 'Don't say you can't, you can,' to verbally encourage him to complete the transition to the horse riding activity.
options.D: Check that Adam has understood what has been communicated by showing him a pair of riding boots, providing an alternative cue to the original message.
